message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 23
Recent Green phase: 
- Re-added `items` to Policy
- Added HIGH_ENCH_PAYOUT_THRESHOLD (8) and HIGH_ENCH_PAYOUT_RATE (0.5)
- Added `reimbursementFor(item, amount)` that returns 50% of amount when enchantment ≥ 8, else amount
- Changed `payoutForDamage` to look up the matching item by type in the policy and apply reimbursementFor before deducting

Refactor the implementation while keeping all tests green.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, return when done.

attachment.content:
- red: TDD Red Phase
- green: TDD Green Phase
- test-list: TDD Test List Phase
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: Now let me look at the recent test that prompted the green phase changes:

The reminder isn't applicable here — this is a single refactoring decision, not a multi-step task. Let me continue with the measurement-driven refactoring.

Looking at the implementation, ESLint reports zero smells. McCabe scores are all low (max is 3 in `reimbursementFor`). The functions are already well-decomposed using table-driven patterns (SURCHARGE_RULES, POLICY_DISCOUNT_RULES).

## Naming Evaluation

Let me evaluate the recently added/modified names:

- `reimbursementFor(item, amount)`: Reveals intent — returns the reimbursable portion of damage for an item. Good.
- `HIGH_ENCH_PAYOUT_THRESHOLD` / `HIGH_ENCH_PAYOUT_RATE`: Slightly abbreviated. Compare to `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD` (line 29) — this uses the full word "ENCHANTMENT". The new constants abbreviate to "ENCH". This is **inconsistent within the same file**.
- `payoutForDamage`: Good.

There's a clear naming improvement: align `HIGH_ENCH_PAYOUT_*` with the existing `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_*` style.

Also, looking at the structure, I notice the codebase uses **rule lists** for surcharges and discounts. The `reimbursementFor` is currently a single conditional — but I should not preemptively generalize to a rule list (Rule 4: Fewest Elements). With only one rule, a rule list adds complexity without benefit.

## Identifying Worst Offender

Looking at McCabe scores:
- `reimbursementFor`: 3 (highest, just added)
- `payoutForDamage`: 2
- `handleStep`: 2
- Everything else: 1

`reimbursementFor` has the highest McCabe at 3, but two of those branches are `??` (default for missing enchantment). McCabe 3 is still very low.

The most impactful refactoring is the **naming consistency fix** — rename `HIGH_ENCH_PAYOUT_*` to `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_PAYOUT_*` to match the existing constant naming pattern.

## Apply Refactoring (ONE change)

content.text:
All 23 tests still passing, ESLint still 0 smells.

## POST-Measurement

**Function `reimbursementFor`** (after rename):
- Constants: 1
- Bindings: 5
- Invocations: 3
- Conditionals: 2
- Mass: 20 (unchanged — only names changed)
- McCabe: 3 (unchanged)

The rename did not affect structural complexity metrics, but it did fix a naming consistency issue (Rule 2: Reveals Intent).

## Final Report

Refactoring Complete:

**Refactoring**: Renamed `HIGH_ENCH_PAYOUT_THRESHOLD` → `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_PAYOUT_THRESHOLD` and `HIGH_ENCH_PAYOUT_RATE` → `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_PAYOUT_RATE` to match the pre-existing `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_*` naming pattern (line 28-29).

**Smell delta (whole file)**: 0 → 0 (already clean)
**Cognitive delta** (target function `reimbursementFor`): below threshold → below threshold (no SonarJS report either pre or post)
**APP mass delta** (`reimbursementFor`): 20 → 20 (no structural change)
**McCabe delta** (`reimbursementFor`): 3 → 3 (no branch change)
**Tests**: 23/23 passing

**Justification for rename without structural change**:
Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) trumps lower-priority rules. The newly-introduced constants used abbreviated `ENCH` while the existing file convention is full `ENCHANTMENT` (see `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD`, `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_SURCHARGE_RATE`). Inconsistent naming forces readers to wonder if `ENCH` means something different from `ENCHANTMENT`. Aligning the names removes that cognitive friction without trading off any other metric.

**Why no further refactoring**:
- ESLint smells: already 0
- Max McCabe in file: 3 (`reimbursementFor`), well below any concerning threshold
- Existing structure already uses table-driven dispatch (`SURCHARGE_RULES`, `POLICY_DISCOUNT_RULES`) for the multi-rule families
- `reimbursementFor` has exactly one rule; abstracting it into a `REIMBURSEMENT_RULES` list now would add elements (Rule 4 violation) for no current benefit. That generalization should be deferred until a second reimbursement rule appears (the todo on line 256-258 hints at a dragon-material rule arriving soon — at that point the rule-list abstraction will earn its keep).

Relevant file: `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-27_14-28-32_claim-office-example-mapping_v6.4-metric-driven-refactor_opus-4-7-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts`
